subject: English
skill: Vocabulary, Context Clues, and Word Relationships

lesson.story_mission: Help the Word Detectives solve clues in classroom notes, stories, and science sentences.
lesson.objective: I can use context clues, synonyms, antonyms, shades of meaning, and multiple-meaning words.
lesson.concept_explanation: Good readers use context clues, synonyms, antonyms, shades of meaning, and multiple-meaning words. They slow down, notice clues in the words or text, and explain how the clue supports the answer.
lesson.common_misconception: Readers may choose a meaning without using nearby clues.
lesson.real_world_connection: Vocabulary strategies help readers understand new words in books, tests, projects, and conversations.
lesson.lesson_summary: Use nearby context, word relationships, strength of meaning, and multiple meanings to choose the word that best fits.
lesson.mini_lesson: Read around the unknown word. Look for examples, definitions, contrasts, or a related word. Then test the meaning in the sentence.

audio.text: context clue, synonym, antonym, shade of meaning, multiple-meaning word
audio.label: Listen

worked_examples.text: Watch: In “The trail was damp after the rain,” after the rain is a clue that damp means slightly wet.
worked_examples.context: The trail was damp after the rain, so our shoes got wet.

audio.text: The trail was damp after the rain, so our shoes got wet.
audio.label: Listen

worked_examples.text: Demo: Happy and joyful are synonyms because they have almost the same meaning.

audio.text: Happy and joyful are synonyms.
audio.label: Listen

hint_ladder: Read before and after the word.
Ask whether the words mean the same, opposite, stronger, or weaker.
Test the answer in the sentence.

guided_practice.sentence: The trail was damp after the rain, so our shoes got wet.
guided_practice.context: The trail was damp after the rain, so our shoes got wet.
guided_practice.prompt: In the sentence, what does damp mean?
hints: Reread the important words.
Use the model or clue before answering.
Check that your answer matches the whole sentence or text.
guided_practice.explanation: The answer is slightly wet because the word relationship or context clue supports it.
guided_practice.feedback: Yes — slightly wet fits the reading clue.

question_audio.text: In the sentence, what does damp mean? Choices: slightly wet, very hot, full of dust.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: The trail was damp after the rain, so our shoes got wet.
audio.label: Listen

misconception_bank.context_clue_ignored: Readers may choose a meaning without using nearby clues.
misconception_bank.synonym_antonym_confusion: Readers may mix up words that mean the same with words that mean the opposite.
misconception_bank.shade_of_meaning_confusion: Readers may miss whether a word is stronger, weaker, or more exact.
misconception_bank.multiple_meaning_confusion: Readers may choose the wrong meaning for a word that has more than one meaning.

story.text: Welcome to Vocabulary, Context Clues, and Word Relationships. In this mission, you will use context clues, synonyms, antonyms, shades of meaning, and multiple-meaning words while helping a reader solve a real classroom task. Listen to the story goal, look for the reading strategy, and Press Start Mission when you are ready.
story.label: Read This Page

lesson.text: This mini lesson teaches you how to use context clues and word relationships to choose meanings precisely. Read the objective, look at the visual model, and think about the watch-out mistake before answering. Press Next to continue after you can say the strategy in your own words.
lesson.label: Read This Page

watch.text: Watch this worked example carefully because it shows the whole thinking path. Step one, read the prompt or passage. Step two, use the model to find the clue. Step three, check that the final answer matches the text before you Press Next to continue.
watch.label: Read This Page

demo.text: This guided demo lets you try the strategy with support. Listen to the example, look at the model, and think about why the answer fits. Press Next to continue when you are ready for independent practice.
demo.label: Read This Page

practice.text: This is your Practice zone for Vocabulary, Context Clues, and Word Relationships. Use Read Question to hear the prompt, then reread the sentence or passage yourself. Choose your answer when you are ready, and Use the hint if you need help.
practice.label: Read This Page

challenge.text: This Challenge zone asks you to apply the same strategy with a little more independence. Use Read Question first, listen for any helpful audio, and check the model before answering. Answer carefully, then continue once your choice matches the text.
challenge.label: Read This Page

checkpoint.text: This Checkpoint shows what you can do after the lesson and challenge. Use Read Question to hear the prompt, then prove your answer with the reading strategy. Answer carefully, then continue to see how your work earns growth credit.
checkpoint.label: Read This Page

badge.text: You reached the badge screen for Vocabulary, Context Clues, and Word Relationships. Think about one strategy you used well, such as rereading, checking a clue, or proving an answer. Press Next to see your profile and keep building your Grade 3 English skills.
badge.label: Read This Page

profile.text: This profile page summarizes your growth for Vocabulary, Context Clues, and Word Relationships. Read your accuracy, check any misconception notes, and notice the recommended next step. Continue to Skill Practice or go back to the hub when you are ready.
profile.label: Read This Page
